channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: How the Devs of ‘Stupid Never Dies’ Captured the Right Amount of 'Funky Zombie Action'
item.content:encoded:
It's easy to get overwhelmed by the number of reveals shown during The Game Awards, but one of the few announcements that resonated with me was this punk-rock, flashy, and colorful music video featuring a zombie and his crush. I'm talking about Stupid Never Dies, created by a brand new studio. GPTRACK50 is the name of said studio, and though it's a new name, the developers behind it are anything but. Headed by President Hiroyuki Kobayashi, a 27-year Capcom veteran, he's supported by developers who've worked on Resident Evil, Devil May Cry, Monster Hunter, Metal Gear Solid, and more.

During our hands-off preview, TechRaptor was given a presentation covering the combat system of Stupid Never Dies: this is referred to as "Funky Zombie Action." After, we had a chance to conduct a Q&A style email interview with Kobayashi-san, so be sure to check out our talk further in the article!

Funky Zombie Action Creates a Dynamic Combat System in Stupid Never Dies

Believe me when I say "Funky Zombie Action" fits Stupid Never Dies to a T. The super stylized, pop-punk aesthetic is the perfect fit for an action game, creating a dazzling display of color and chaos. Said chaos is brought about by Stupid Never Dies' many monsters, of which our protagonist, Davy, can take the form of.

In the world of Stupid Never Dies, zombies are at the bottom of the monster hierarchy. Even so, this doesn't stop Davy from jumping into the fray to save the girl he loves. After stumbling across a frozen-solid damsel in distress (Julia), he falls in love and sets out to resurrect her. To resurrect Julia, Davy needs to defeat the King of Monsters (KOM).

The standard gameplay loop revolves around runs in a time-based dungeon filled to the brim with monsters to crush. By himself, Davy is relatively weak (he is, after all, a zombie), but with those newfound powers, he can consume and steal abilities from other monsters. 

In doing so, he becomes that very monster in combat, which allows players to dynamically switch between forms on the fly. There are 11 total forms called "styles," and each style appears to drastically change Davy's move set. To obtain a style, Davy first needs to perform a "Stupid Parry," which exposes a monster's core. Davy can then consume the core and transform into that monster.

This is an exciting system, if anything because of the design of each style. During the presentation, each one seemed to get more and more ridiculous and fun, and the variety here is pretty impressive. Of the 11 different styles, we have: Zombie, Werewolf, Harpy, Golem, Vampire, Will-o'-the-Wisp, Cyclops, Snow Fairy, Merfolk, Lich, and Demon. One neat detail about styles is that each one still retains some elements of Davy's punk outfit.

GPTRACK50 President Hiroyuki Kobayashi's favorite style is the werewolf. This is a style that focuses more on speed, with large swipes from the werewolf's claws. The werewolf can even conjure up a whirlwind-type flurry of blows, so it seems perfect for taking on larger groups of enemies.

Conversely, we have the harpy. The gameplay clip showed off the ability to fly and shoot feathers from a longer distance, and seemed slower -- but no less useful -- compared to the werewolf. 

Some of the other styles are self-explanatory, with the golem being more tanky and the cyclops having larger, sweeping, hard-hitting blows. Some styles appear more technical than others, such as the Will-o'-the-Wisp, which switches from the physical to astral plane so as to avoid damage; meanwhile, the Snow Fairy utilizes a sweet-looking ice sword and can freeze enemies and "make their power yours."

It's hard to say how the Styles will feel in the hands of the player until we actually get to play it for ourselves. Switching between styles is quick and seamless, but our assumption is all 11 powers can't be used in a single run. 

Based on our interview below, we learned that more powerful versions of the Styles, with new and improved abilities, can be acquired later on in runs. Either way, GPTRACK50 is vague on details concerning actual runs through the dungeon and Stupid Never Dies' metaprogression, with promises to reveal more at a later date. 

Outside of styles, however, Stupid Never Dies features a "body hack" system which allows Davy to modify parts of his body with gear. Zombie style is the one we're going to use the most, so to add more flavor to Davy's toolkit, body hacking allows for a more diverse set of skills. 

Some examples include attaching a gravity launcher to his arm, which shoots a projectile that'll group enemies together. There's also the massive edge, which is this sword about the same size as Davy himself; by attaching it to his hand, he can sweep through large crowds of enemies.

And to emphasize the idea that Stupid Never Dies is all about "Funky Zombie Action," GPTRACK50 showcased "Davy Burst," which is essentially his special move. In an explosion of psychedelic color, Davy gets a power boost and we see a clip of him absolutely demolishing a group of enemies.

Of course, there's so much more to Stupid Never Dies that we have yet to see, including dungeon runs and how progression works, but what's showcased here is exciting. Really, it's the sheer variety of different forms that gives me a great deal of confidence that combat won't get stale, and that it's mechanically rich enough to appeal to even hardcore action gamers. That, combined with GPTRACK50 comprising a bunch of veteran developers, instills hope that Stupid Never Dies will be a excellent action title.

GPTRACK50 President Hiroyuki Kobayashi on Making Monsters in Stupid Never Dies

TechRaptor: There are so many different monsters you could choose to use for styles, so can you talk about your reasoning on why you chose some of the styles you did? (e.g., Vampires, Liches, Demons, Will-o’-the-Wisp, and more)

Hiroyuki Kobayashi: From the many monsters available, we selected ones that best support different player playstyles.

Within the team, we discussed and chose monsters that fit various combat approaches, such as close-range, mid-range, long-range, speed-focused, power-focused, flying, and even underwater types.

TR: Each style has their own set of moves, based on the design of the monster you’re taking the form of. Were there any specific action games that influenced the move set of certain monsters? What was your design philosophy for these moves?

HK: When designing each style, we looked at techniques from various action games, but our primary focus was on how to reflect the imagery and characteristics that gamers associate with those classic monsters.

In terms of the philosophy behind the move design, we wanted to fully embrace the protagonist’s identity as a zombie. The idea is essentially: “Even if you lose two or three arms, don’t worry about it -- forget defense and keep attacking!”

Our goal is to create funky, aggressive action gameplay where constantly pushing the attack feels powerful and delivers a strong sense of catharsis for the player.

TR: Zombie form is said to be the foundation of all other styles. Is the Zombie style able to hold its own in battle? How often are you using Zombie style compared to other forms Davy can take on?

HK: The Zombie Style is fully viable in combat. However, taking on stronger monsters will require a certain level of technique.

At the start of every dungeon, the player always begins in Zombie Style, and since it is also used in combination with other styles, it will likely be the style players use most frequently.

TR: How did you navigate the development of 11 total styles, assuring that each style fit their own niche and role in the combat of Stupid Never Dies?

HK: The Zombie Style was designed as a standard character that is easy to handle. In contrast, the other characters are meant to be extremely powerful when used in the right situations -- but much tougher to use if the conditions don’t fit.

To achieve this balance, we first considered the types of pressure a player might face from individual enemies, as well as the situations that could arise from different enemy combinations. Based on that, we determined what kinds of situations each style should excel in.

From there, we designed each style’s actions, attack range, movement speed, attack speed and area, and even character size to ensure they could effectively break through those specific situations.

TR: From a gameplay perspective, were any styles more challenging than others to develop a cohesive combat style for? Can you explain what hurdles you faced in developing so many diverse styles for Davy to take the form of?

HK: The Harpy Style and Will-o’-the-Wisp Style, which excel at long-range combat, were particularly challenging to design.

From the early stages of development, we had certain assumptions about the ideal number of enemies, the appropriate combat distance, and the right level of pressure during battles. However, as development progressed, many of these elements only became clear through the process itself.

TR: Stupid Never Dies is fast-paced and heavy on the action, and you can switch between different styles to fit your needs in combat. How are you making it so that switching between styles doesn’t break the flow of battle?

HK: With the exception of clearly committed moments, players are allowed to cancel most actions by switching styles. We also designed the style-change action to be quick and fluid so that it does not disrupt the overall tempo of the game.

During development, we experimented with more elaborate visual transitions, but in the end we settled on the current instant-style switching, which felt the most responsive.

For example, players can use the Merfolk Style to dive and rapidly close the distance of an enemy, then immediately switch to Golem Style to deliver a powerful strike.

This kind of combat wouldn’t be possible using the Golem Style alone, since it moves very slowly.

By enabling instant style switching, we designed the system so that players can compensate for each style’s weaknesses while amplifying their strengths, allowing for a wide range of creative play combinations.

TR: What are some ways in which Stupid Never Dies encourages players to use different styles in combat, rather than sticking to using one the whole time?

HK: For example, even within the Werewolf Style, the skills you obtain will vary depending on which werewolf you Style Eat.

If you Style Eat a stronger werewolf found in the deeper layers of the dungeon, you can acquire a more powerful version of the Werewolf Style.

We encourage players to switch styles actively, choosing the one that best matches their strategy, the skills that complement their current deck, or the style that best suits the immediate combat situation.

TR: Davy himself can be upgraded with various body hacks, but are there ways that we can also enhance the abilities of the different styles in Stupid Never Dies?

HK: The game features both temporary progression and permanent progression systems.

Temporary progression includes elements such as selecting rarities that suit your current style during a run.

As for the permanent progression systems, please stay tuned for upcoming details.

TR: I also want to touch on The Game Awards reveal. As a new studio, what were your thoughts leading up to the reveal and do you have any comments on the reception for the trailer? 

HK: Right before the announcement, we were very nervous. Watching it actually be revealed at The Game Awards, our main feeling was, “We finally did it -- GPTRACK50’s first title has been announced.” It has been a long journey to get to this point.

Thanks to the support of the media, we feel the trailer was successfully shared with audiences around the world. We were especially surprised -- and very happy -- to see such a strong response from South America and Central Asia.

TR: I actually listened to the trailer on repeat, because I really enjoyed the pop/punk-style song. I’m curious about why you chose to go with a song like this and how it is representative of the game itself? Can we expect more music like this in the final product?

HK: Many action games tend to feature rock-style music, so when creating a new IP, we wanted to challenge ourselves by exploring a different musical direction.

When thinking about the brand identity of this title, we came up with several keywords based on the game’s themes: emo, street culture, speed, youth, and recklessness. From there, we decided that the music should lean toward “pop punk.”

TR: Is there anything else you would like people to know about Stupid Never Dies that hasn't been asked?

HK: With a brand-new title, it’s incredibly challenging to first get people to know about it and then get them to actually play it. We would be truly grateful if action game fans and members of the media could support this title, Stupid Never Dies, and help us spread the word.

Thank you very much for your support!!



https://techraptor.net/gaming/interviews/how-devs-behind-stupid-never-dies-injected-just-right-amount-of-funky-zombie
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Star Wars Unlimited A Lawless Time Booster Box Opening - Underworld Shakeup
item.content:encoded:
Star Wars is famous for its force wielders on both sides, but the franchise is also full of iconic characters who don't use the force and aren't always on the side of the law. Smugglers, criminals, and outlaws fill the galaxy, and the latest expansion for the Star Wars Unlimited CCG, A Lawless Time, focuses on these characters, along with bringing some changes to how individual booster packs are made up. 

In this article, we'll talk about the changes and then showcase the cards we pulled from a booster box of the brand-new expansion.

We've been covering Star Wars Unlimited since its release, and you can check our our Star Wars Unlimited Guide, and our review of the original set.

Everything Changes

With the release of A Lawless Time, Star Wars Unlimited has some changes to how card standard packs are made up:

  • Standard foils have been removed entirely, and instead, every pack will have a Hyperspace foil (a foil full art card)
  • Each pack will also contain at least 1 Hyperspace card (previously 1 in every 2 packs)
  • Standard packs will now also contain non-foil prestige cards, alternative art cards that are in 1 of every 18 packs
  • They also reduced the pack distribution for rare and legendary variants, so you should now see more hyperspace and hyperspace foils in packs

There are also some changes to the Carbonite Booster Packs, more expensive packs that give a better rarity and showcase return, and the full list of changes can be seen here.

In our A Lawless Time Booster Box opening below, we're going to show all the cards we got in the packs we opened, to give a rough idea of how booster boxes are made up now. We opened all 24 booster packs in the box, and we'll show all cards that aren't standard common or uncommon.

Booster Pack 1

Our very first pack opened straight into a Legendary, with Maul - Master of the Shadow Collective. Maul has the ability to be a very powerful board control card, as he can take control of one of your opponents' non-leader units if he deals damage to their base. With overwhelm, that means that even when blocked, he will still deal damage to their base, so they're going to want to stop all of that damage and take Maul out as quickly as possible. 

We also see the new card pack breakdown, with us getting at least one Hyperspace and one Hyperspace foil.

Booster Pack 2

Our next pack is solid rarity-wise, as we got a Hyperspace Rare in an uncommon slot, along with a Legendary card in our Hyperspace Foil slot. 

Liberty Draw Their Fire is a huge card, in terms of cost and influence on the board. Your opponent will have to attack it first before your base due to Sentinel. And you get an automatic benefit when it's played, and when it attacks, as it can exhaust an enemy unit and return all upgrades that cost 4 or less to their owners' hands, forcing your opponent to pay to play them again. This lets you lock down an enemy threat the turn it arrives.

Booster Pack 3

Our third pack wasn't big in terms of rarity, but we benefitted from the new Hyperspace distribution, getting a Hyperspace Uncommon, a Common in our Hyperspace Foil slot, and an additional Hyperspace Common.

I do like the Rare we got with Krrsantan. Krr is a pricey unit, so won't see play early game, and for the cost of discarding 2 cards, you can return them to your hand. This might seem counterintuitive for such a pricey unit, but with Ambush and Overwhelm, it means that they can attack the turn they hit the board, pick their target, and overwhelm a unit, then discard 2 cards to return them to your hand, which means they return to the board with no damage when you play them again. It's a pricey play in terms of cost and discarding cards, but late-game Krr can keep smashing over units if you have the resources.

Booster Pack 4

Our fourth pack was a sad pack for pulls. A single standard Rare and a Common Hyperspace Foil. Double-Cross is an interesting card, but not mind-blowing. The ability to steal an enemy unit can have a solid impact, but it's a high-cost event that relies on you giving your opponent a useless unit in exchange for a useful one.

Booster Pack 5

Our fifth pack was another sad pack for pulls with another single standard Rare and a Common Hyperspace Foil. I do love Vult Skerris's Defender though, as being able to deal 1 damage and exhaust a space unit is huge. It can lock out some huge units, or force your opponent to attack out of sequence to avoid the Defender locking out their unit.

Booster Pack 6

Our sixth pack was another big draw pack, with 2 Rares, a Common Hyperspace Foil, another Hyperspace Common, and our Base was a Hyperspace Common. So not big on value, but 5 of the 16 cards were above normal Commons/Uncommons.

I love the themamtic nature of Display Piece. It removes a unit from play and then places that unit in your opponent's resource stack, putting them on display for all to see. I think the unit should be kept face up to show that they are now secure in carbonite. Early game it will help your opponent ramp up, but late game this is an effective removal tool.

Booster Pack 7

Our seventh pack was an odd pack. We got a base in our Rare slot, which FFG has said won't happen in the set after A Lawless Time. We also got a Rare leader, and a Hyperspace Foil Common, and a Hyperspace Common.

I like the Citadel Research Centre base card. Usually when you resource a card from your hand, it's gone from the game, but this allows you to still use them, pulling it to your hand and replacing it with the top card of your deck. This has the added bonus of letting you have a bit more control of your draw as well, pulling useful cards from your resource and cycling your top deck to find what you need.

Booster Pack 8

Our eighth pack saw us again get a Base in our Rare slot, but we got a Hyperspace Foil Rare in the foil slot to offset it (another copy of Display Piece - see above). We also got a Hyperspace Common and a Hyperspace Base.

I like Daring Delve in this pack. This common lets you cycle your deck, very helpful if you're playing an aggressive red aggression aspect, which everyone knows is my preferred style of play.

Booster Pack 9

Our ninth pack saw us pull a Rare in our Leader slot, a Hyperspace Base, a Hyperspace Common, and a Common in the foil slot, along with the standard Rare.

I like the Haxion Aggressor here in the foil slot. 2 cost, 4 power in attack. Let's go!

Booster Pack 10

In our tenth pack we pulled  Legendary in our Rare slot, a Hyperspace Uncommon, and an additional Hyperspace Common alongside the Hyperspace Foil Common.

I love the thinking behind Tear This Ship Apart, the Legendary we pulled. Being able to pull one of your opponent's cards that they've used as a resource for 7 cost means that there should be a decent choice of cards down by the time you can afford it, but getting the 7 cost return back will be very hit or miss and I don't think the 7 cost will see much play as it's super risky as you don't even pull the resource from them as they top deck to replace it. I would much rather they lost the resource as well to offset the risk that they've used a load of low-cost cards as resources.

Booster Pack 11

Our eleventh pack was marginally above standard with a single Rare, a Common Hyperspace Foil, and an additional Hyperspace Common.

The Rare, Rose Tico Now It's Worth It is a solid card, but for me the Hyperspace Kage Elite is my favorite card as a 3 cost 4 attack (with the Raid ability) and Sabotuer that rips the shields from units, and allows you to ignore the Sentinel keyword to head straight for the base, is the kind of aggro card that I love.

Booster Pack 12

Another slightly above average pack with our twelfth booster pack, pulling a Rare, a Common Hypespace Foil, an additional Hyperspace Common, and a Hyperspace Common Leader.

Ghost is a great 6 cost, 4/4 ship that can give itself or another unit +1/+1 and a shield, and if you control a Vigilance or Aggression aspect unit, 2x +1/+1 and 2 shields. It does have 3 Aspects of its own, so it should see play in multi-aspect decks (like Leia's Spotlight deck in this set), making use of Daimyo's Palace to ignore aspects for costs, but benefitting from Leia's ability to add +1/+1's per aspect on cards.

Booster Pack 13

Our thirteenth pack was again slightly above average, with a single Rare, Hyperspace Foil Common, and an additional Hyperspace Common.

I like Eney's Nest's Helmet, our Rare here, as it gives a unit +2 hit points, plus when you attack with it, it gives another unit +3 power for that phase, which can set up some great strikes, or make your opponent think twice about attacking what was previously a weak unit.

Booster Pack 14

Pack fourteen saw a better draw for us, with our next Legendary card, a Hyperspace Uncommon, a Hyperspace Foil Common, and an additional Hyperspace Common.

The Hondo Legendary is a great card, but I absolutely love my Flash The Vents Hyperspace Foil here. A low-cost, +2 attack with overwhelm, with the downside that if the overwhelm damage goes through the unit is defeated, isn't really a downside as most aggro decks are full of low-cost, low HP units that rarely survive anyway.

Booster Pack 15

Pack fifteen is another slightly above-average draw, with a Rare, Common Hyperspace Foil and additional Hyperspace Common.

The 3-cost Collateral Damage reminds me slightly of Chain Lightning from Magic the Gathering, so it rates high for me, but the multi-aspect Zuckuss is also very decent and makes me want to experiment with a multi-aspect Leia Bounty Hunter deck.

Booster Pack 16

Pack sixteen saw us draw our first Special Card with the Sarlaxx of Carkoon. This card already comes with 3 copies in the Jabba spotlight deck from this set, but if you haven't picked that up, you may come across Special cards in booster packs too now. We also got a standard Rare, a Common Hyperspace Foil, an additional Hyperspace Common, and a Hyperspace Common Base.

I love both Baze and the Sarlacc here. We'll discuss the Sarlacc in our Spotlight decks review, so I'll focus on Baze. Baze is a high-cost multi-aspect card, who, if you can set up with healing abilities, can do some serious work as your opponent will be forced to attack this Sentinel unit, and you can just dish out damage as you heal them, without ever having to even attack with them.

Booster Pack 17

My view of above-average has become what I think is average for A Lawless time, with a Rare, Hyperspace Foil Common and an additional Common, as we see again in our seventeenth pack.

I like Anakin Skywalker Prescient Podracer here, as a low-cost unit with some great synergies to heal your base, and bounce units back to your hand to heal, or gain the play benefit again.

Booster Pack 18

The standard card draw for a Lawless Time for pack eighteen with a Rare, Common Hyperspace Foil, and a Hyperspace Common.

All the cards on show here have a Credits theme, with one generating credits, another generating credits and then dealing damage equal to your credits, and the Rare, the Conveyex Security Captain, locking down on underworld dealings by removing the abilities of credits while in play.

Booster Pack 19

Pack nineteen saw some improvements in draw with a Legendary, Hyperspace Uncommon, Common Foil Hyperspace, and a Rare leader.

The standout card here is obviously Like Skywalker Profit Or Be Destroyed, who is a 7 cost 3-aspect card, with 9 power for some serious damage dealing, and a when played ability that your opponent chooses, which is either Luke deals 5 damage to a unit, or your opponent gains a credit token and Luke readies and can attack that turn.

Booster Pack 20

Pack twenty sees an above-average draw as we close on the end of the booster box, with a Rare, a Hyperspace Uncommon, a Common Hyperspace Foil, and a Hyperspace Common.

As a huge fan of the Andor series, Cassian is my pick here, and he could add some real power to a low-cost aggro deck, as if a unit is defeated after it attacks, Andor deals 2 damage to your opponent's base, letting you ramp up some real damage across a turn.

Booster Pack 21

Pack twenty-one sees us draw a Rare, A Hyperspace Foil Common, a Hyperspace Common, and a Hyperspace Common Base.

Fire Across The Galaxy, which lets you use any number of When Played abilities on Spectre units as a 6-cost action just screams combo deck, and I can't wait to hear some crazy stories of game-ending combos if players can pull this card off.

Booster Pack 22

Pack twenty-two sees another above-average draw, with a Rare, a Hyperspace Foil Uncommon, 2 Hyperspace Commons, and a Rare Leader.

I love the thematic nature of Fett's Firespray here, creating credit tokens when defeating units, and as a 6 cost, 4/6 Ambusher, it's a solid card.

Booster Pack 23

Pack 23 saw another slightly above average draw, with a Rare, an Uncommon Hyperspace Foil, a Hyperspace Uncommon, and a Hyperspace Common.

The Rare, Rio Durant Beckett's Right Hands was an awesome draw here because I then drew the Rare Leader Tobias Beckett in the next pack, which was awesome. Rio lets you do some shenanigans with returning a non-leader card that costs less than 3 to your hand (or opponent's if you wish), and they can play it for free with Shielded for this phase. This will remove any damage from a unit, and also grant you the When Played ability on that unit again.

Booster Pack 24

I didn't have high hopes for our final pack, pack 24, but it delivered, giving us a Legendary, a Rare Prestige card (which we discussed in the intro), a Common Hyperspace Foil, a Hyperspace Common, and a Rare Leader.

The Prestige card we drew here, whilst not as awesome as the limited number Prestige Foils in Carbonite packs, is still pretty awesome and has great artwork. This 2 cost, 2/2 ship allows you to chain play cards and also play (paying its cost) another Heroism unit from your hand. I also like the concept behind the Intimidator Legendary we pulled here. It's a high 11-cost ship with 11/11 stats, but it lets you return any number of resources to your hand, creating a credit token for each returned this way. This means that if you drop it with 11 resources down, you get 11 credits with which to play those 11 cards you just drew into your hand, but you will have to start building up resources again to play next turn. I do love how this could be a huge all-in impact card, but if it doesn't pull off, you're pretty much done.

Summary

Here are the stats for our first A Lawless Time Booster Box:

  • Legendary - 6 (including 1 Hyperspace Foil)
  • Rare - 28 (including 1 Prestige, 1 Hyperspace Foil, 2 Hyperspace, 5 Leaders (including 1 Hyperspace Leader), and 2 Bases)
  • 1 Special Card
  • Uncommon beyond normal - 8 (including 2 Hypespace Foils, and 6 Hyperspace)
  • Common beyond normal - 48 (including 20 Hyperspace Foils, 21 Hyperspace, 2 Hyperspace Leaders, 5 Hyperspace Bases)

How did our booster box shape up against your first A Lawless Time booster box? Did you get the cards you want, and what decks are you building with the new expansion? Let us know in the comments.

The copy of the Star Wars Unlimited A Lawless Time Booster Box used to produce this guide was provided by Asmodee UK.



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/star-wars-unlimited-lawless-time-booster-box-opening-underworld-shakeup
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: New Cambridge Program May Have Identified Rules For Ancient Board Game
item.content:encoded:
Cambridge University may have learned the rules of an ancient board game. In a recent academic report, the university utilized a new computer program to analyze a game board piece in the Netherlands, resulting in a potential discovery of ancient tabletop play.

Cambridge University, the Ludii System, and the Ancient Board Game

The Cambridge University report on the ancient board game is extensively academic, but intriguing. According to the report, a piece of rounded limestone was uncovered at a Roman site of Coriovallum in Heerlen, The Netherlands, which was identified as a game board of some kind. But the marks on the board didn't match any known kind of game from that era.

The report then goes into detail about ancient board game design. It remarks that certain popular games in various regions were played with drawing the "board" in the sand and game pieces in the form of sticks, stones, or shells. Comparatively, Roman games are the most documented, since they involved game pieces, game boards made of stone, and even the rolling of dice.

This is where the new game board games into place. The marks on the board don't match any known patterns on other similar artifacts uncovered, and there are no instruction manuals. So, how was this particular board game played? The report asserts that the answer will be found in use-wear analysis and the university's Ludii general game system.

Use-wear analysis, in laymen's terms, is identifying wear on an item through repeated movement against different surfaces. This happened a lot in Roman games since game pieces were tiny dome or disc-shaped counters made out of glass or stone. This meant sliding the pieces across the stone game board was common, wearing down the shown with abrasive scratches.

This means that the size and frequency of use-wear on a game board can determine the movement and placement patterns of the game pieces, which can inform the potential cadence and pattern of play. A good example of this is the difference between movements of a Chess board and Sternhalma (aka "Chinese Checkers").

The second part comes from the Ludii system. The program was developed as part of the Digital Ludeme Project, and allows for thorough and fast implementation and analysis of ancient board game rules as part of archeological research. 

Through machine learning, the Ludii program looked at the use-wear of the board, consulted the rules of similar board games found throughout Europe within its database, and then playing out games between two different opponents with different rules systems to see which one best matched the board.

According to Ludii's findings, there were two broad kinds of board game that could have been played on this board. The first is a blocking game similar to Hare and Hounds, or Go. The second is an alignment game, where players have to spend turns trying to get three of their game pieces in a row.

The details were more daunting. The line patterns lead to six different board configurations,  and 130 different game configurations including starting positions, legal moves, and game-ending moves. 112 of those configurations were variations on Hare and Hounds.

Overall, Cambridge's report on ancient board games is fascinating. It's a showcase of the level of both historical and cultural research that has gone into the hobby of board games that such informed decisions can be made with so little material. It also demonstrates how, even back then, variations and remixes of familiar rules were happening all the time.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/new-cambridge-program-may-have-identified-rules-for-ancient-board-game
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
